barcode_source_note: Not found/verified in this session – barcodes vary by pack size and change over time; scan the physical package or check GS1 India / Open Food Facts to confirm.
india_ingredient_1: Wheat, milk solids, added sugar (~2.7g per serving average across 15 Indian variants), other cereals, vitamins and minerals (iron, zinc, vitamins A/C/D)
india_ingredient_2: Wheat, milk solids, other cereals, vitamins and minerals (iron, zinc, vitamins A/C/D) - no added sugar
india_ingredient_3: A 2024 Public Eye/IBFAN investigation found all 15 Cerelac variants tested in India contained added sugar (avg. 2.7g/serving), while the same Cerelac cereals sold in the UK, Germany and Switzerland for babies 6+ months contained none.
india_ingredient_6: FSSAI permits up to 20% added sugar in infant cereals under Codex-aligned rules; Nestle voluntarily removed added sugar from equivalent products in the EU/UK. WHO recommends no added sugar in foods for children under 2.
india_ingredient_7: Early exposure to added sugar in infant food can encourage a preference for sweet tastes and is linked by pediatric nutrition bodies to higher risk of childhood obesity and dental caries; Nestle India announced reformulation to remove added sugar following the report.
india_ingredient_8: Public Eye investigation (publiceye.ch), The Week, Business Standard, India TV News, April 2024

consumer_note: Wheat: Allowed everywhere; must be declared as a gluten-containing allergen on labels in most jurisdictions. | Milk Solids: No regulatory concerns; standard dairy ingredient, must be declared as an allergen. | Added Sugar (~2.7g per serving average across 15 Indian variants): Allowed under FSSAI's Codex-aligned limit in India, but WHO recommends no added sugar for children under 2, and Nestle voluntarily removed it from equivalent EU/UK/Swiss products after public scrutiny; Nestle India announced reformulation to remove added sugar following the 2024 report. | Vitamins and Minerals (Iron, Zinc, Vitamins A/C/D): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities.

barcode_source_note: Verified – Open Food Facts (Maggi Masala 2-Minute Noodles, Nestlé India, 70g); ingredient list matches CSV row (hydrolysed groundnut protein, masala tastemaker, colour 150d).
country_compared: Multiple markets (typical Maggi noodle formulations outside India)
india_ingredient_1: Wheat flour (Maida), edible vegetable oil, salt, wheat gluten, mixed spices, hydrolysed groundnut protein, onion powder, colour (150d); Nestle states no added MSG (E621)
india_ingredient_2: Wheat flour, vegetable oil, salt, spices, and Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG) added directly, per typical non-India Maggi noodle seasoning formulations
india_ingredient_3: Nestle's own global Q&A confirms Maggi Noodles made in India do not have added MSG (E621), relying instead on natural glutamate from hydrolysed groundnut protein and onion powder; many other-country Maggi seasoning formulations list added MSG directly as Flavour Enhancer 621.
india_ingredient_4: Natural glutamate sources (hydrolysed groundnut protein), Colour 150d (caramel), Flavour Enhancer 621 (non-India variants)
india_ingredient_5: Wheat, Gluten, Peanut (groundnut protein), may contain milk (shared line)
india_ingredient_6: Following India's 2015 lead/MSG controversy and nationwide recall, Nestle India agreed to remove the 'No added MSG' label claim while maintaining the product never contained added MSG; courts cleared the product as safe in October 2015 after re-testing.
india_ingredient_7: MSG is classified Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) by the US FDA and is approved for use by FSSAI; a small subset of people report short-term sensitivity symptoms such as headache or flushing.
india_ingredient_8: Nestle Global Ask Nestle Q&A (nestle.com), Maggi.in, Wikipedia 'Maggi', eathealthy365.com
ingredient_explanation: Wheat Flour (Maida) [Wheat Flour / Maida (refined wheat flour)]: Milled wheat grain, refined (maida) or whole; provides starch and gluten structure. | Edible Vegetable Oil [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Mixed Spices [Onion Powder / Garlic Powder / Spices]: Dried, ground allium vegetables and spice blends for savoury flavour. | Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein [Hydrolysed Vegetable Protein (HVP)]: Plant protein (often soy, wheat, or groundnut) broken down by acid/enzymes to release natural glutamate for savoury flavour, without adding isolated MSG. | Colour (150d) [Caramel Colour, Class IV / Sulphite Ammonia Caramel (150d/INS150d)]: A brown colourant made by heating sugar with ammonium and sulphite compounds; gives cola drinks their dark colour. | Vegetable Oil [Vegetable Oil (Corn/Soybean/Canola/Sunflower blend)]: A blend of seed oils used as a frying/cooking medium. | Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG) [Monosodium Glutamate (MSG) / INS or E621]: The sodium salt of glutamic acid, a naturally-occurring amino acid; delivers the savoury 'umami' taste.
ingredient_function: Wheat Flour (Maida): Base/structuring ingredient (Natural) | Edible Vegetable Oil: Frying oil/fat source (Natural) | Salt: Seasoning/preservative (Natural) | Mixed Spices: Flavouring (Natural) | Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein: Natural flavour/umami source (Natural (processed)) | Colour (150d): Colouring agent (Natural (processed)) | Vegetable Oil: Frying oil/fat source (Natural) | Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG): Flavour enhancer (Natural (fermentation-derived))
india_regulation: Wheat Flour (Maida): Allowed | Edible Vegetable Oil: Allowed | Salt: Allowed | Mixed Spices: Allowed | Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein: Allowed | Colour (150d): Allowed | Vegetable Oil: Allowed | Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG): Allowed-Warning

banned_country: None identified in the 8 reviewed jurisdictions
restricted_country: Colour (150d): California (US) requires a cancer-warning label under Proposition 65 if the by-product 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI) exceeds a set threshold - a state-level, not federal, requirement
regulatory_status: Wheat Flour (Maida): Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Edible Vegetable Oil: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Salt: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Mixed Spices: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Colour (150d): Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Vegetable Oil: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG): Restricted/Warning-label required in at least one reviewed jurisdiction
health_concern: Wheat Flour (Maida): Contains gluten; relevant only for those with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. | Edible Vegetable Oil: Higher in saturated fat than sunflower/canola/corn oil blends; frequent consumption adds more saturated fat to the diet. | Salt: High sodium intake is linked to elevated blood pressure risk; WHO recommends under 5g salt/day. | Mixed Spices: None identified. | Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein: May contain trace 3-MCPD/chloropropanols from acid hydrolysis, which EFSA has set limits for in some products; otherwise no acute concern. | Colour (150d): 4-MEI, a trace by-product of Class IV caramel colour manufacture, has been classified as a possible carcinogen by some assessments at high doses in animal studies; typical dietary exposure is well below levels of concern per JECFA/EFSA. | Vegetable Oil: Higher in unsaturated fat than palm oil; generally considered a favourable substitute for saturated fats. | Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG): Classified Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) by FDA and reviewed favourably by JECFA/EFSA; a small subset of sensitive individuals report transient symptoms (headache, flushing) after large intakes.
consumer_note: Wheat Flour (Maida): Allowed everywhere; must be declared as a gluten-containing allergen on labels in most jurisdictions. | Edible Vegetable Oil: Allowed everywhere; the choice of palm oil versus other vegetable oils is a manufacturer cost decision, not a regulatory requirement. | Salt: Allowed in India but restricted in some countries' voluntary reformulation programmes due to concerns over excessive sodium consumption. | Mixed Spices: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein: Allowed in India but restricted in some countries due to trace-contaminant limits (3-MCPD) set for the hydrolysis process rather than the ingredient itself. | Colour (150d): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities at normal consumption levels; California uniquely requires a warning label above a specific 4-MEI threshold. | Vegetable Oil: No regulatory concerns identified. | Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG): Allowed in India (and worldwide) but India is one of the few countries that still requires a specific pregnancy/infant caution label on MSG-containing products.
verified_sources: Wheat Flour (Maida): Codex Alimentarius allergen labelling; general knowledge | Edible Vegetable Oil: General nutrition science; FSSAI has no oil-type mandate for snacks | Salt: WHO sodium guideline | Mixed Spices: General food knowledge | Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein: EFSA opinion on 3-MCPD in food (general knowledge) | Colour (150d): California Prop 65 4-MEI listing; JECFA/EFSA caramel colour reviews | Vegetable Oil: General nutrition science | Flavour Enhancer (621 / MSG): FSSAI GMP-based approval; discovermsg.com summary of FSSAI position; FDA GRAS status; JECFA/EFSA reviews (ADI 30mg/kg bw, EFSA 2017)

barcode_source_note: Likely match – listed on in.openfoodfacts.org (India OFF instance) as Cadbury Dairy Milk Chocolate 24g; Mondelez uses shared global GS1 prefixes, so confirm against physical pack before relying on it.
india_ingredient_1: Sugar, milk solids (minimum 22%), cocoa butter, cocoa solids, emulsifiers (442, 476), flavours
india_ingredient_2: Milk, sugar, cocoa butter, cocoa mass, vegetable fats (palm, shea), emulsifiers (E442, E476), flavourings; milk solids 20% minimum (actual 23%)
india_ingredient_3: The UK bar contains added vegetable fats (palm and/or shea) alongside cocoa butter, permitted under EU chocolate rules (up to 5% non-cocoa vegetable fat); India's classic Dairy Milk bar is formulated with cocoa butter as the only fat plus a slightly higher minimum milk solids threshold, per Cadbury India's own labelling.
india_ingredient_4: Emulsifiers E442 (ammonium phosphatide) and E476 (PGPR), vegetable fats (UK variant only)
india_ingredient_5: Milk; may contain nuts and wheat (shared manufacturing lines)
india_ingredient_6: EU/UK chocolate standards (Directive 2000/36/EC) permit up to 5% vegetable fat substitution for cocoa butter in milk chocolate; India's FSSAI standards for 'milk chocolate' allow this too, but Cadbury's standard India bar does not currently use it, unlike the UK bar.
india_ingredient_7: Vegetable fat substitutes such as palm and shea are higher in saturated fat than cocoa butter; the practical health difference at typical chocolate serving sizes is minor compared to the bar's overall sugar content (56g sugar per 100g in the UK bar).
india_ingredient_8: British Corner Shop / British Essentials product labels, Cadbury Dairy Milk Wikipedia entry, Quora (sourced from Cadbury regional packaging), THIP Media fact-check
ingredient_explanation: Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Milk Solids (minimum 22%) [Milk Solids / Milk Powder]: Dried milk components (protein, lactose, fat) used to add dairy richness. | Cocoa Butter [Cocoa Solids / Cocoa Mass / Cocoa Butter]: Components derived from roasted cocoa beans. | Emulsifiers (442, 476) [Ammonium Phosphatides (E442/INS442) & Polyglycerol Polyricinoleate / PGPR (E476/INS476)]: Emulsifiers that reduce the amount of cocoa butter needed and improve chocolate's flow/viscosity during moulding. | Flavours [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea) [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction.
ingredient_function: Sugar: Sweetener (Natural) | Milk Solids (minimum 22%): Dairy base/texture (Natural) | Cocoa Butter: Flavour/fat base for chocolate (Natural) | Emulsifiers (442, 476): Emulsifier (Artificial (E442) / semi-synthetic from castor oil (E476)) | Flavours: Flavouring (Varies (Natural / Nature-identical / Artificial, per label)) | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea): Frying oil/fat source (Natural)
india_regulation: Sugar: Allowed | Milk Solids (minimum 22%): Allowed | Cocoa Butter: Allowed | Emulsifiers (442, 476): Allowed | Flavours: Allowed | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea): Allowed

banned_country: None identified in the 8 reviewed jurisdictions
restricted_country: Emulsifiers (442, 476): EU sets maximum use levels for PGPR in chocolate/cocoa products under Annex II of Regulation (EC) 1333/2008
regulatory_status: Sugar: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Milk Solids (minimum 22%): Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Cocoa Butter: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Emulsifiers (442, 476): Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Flavours: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea): Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions
health_concern: Sugar: High intake linked to obesity and dental caries; WHO recommends limiting free sugars. | Milk Solids (minimum 22%): Milk is a major declared allergen; relevant for those with dairy allergy or lactose intolerance. | Cocoa Butter: None beyond typical chocolate sugar/fat content. | Emulsifiers (442, 476): Reviewed as safe within EFSA's established Acceptable Daily Intake for PGPR; no major health concerns identified for either substance at typical chocolate-use levels. | Flavours: None identified; standard flavouring practice, though labelling terminology (natural vs nature-identical vs artificial) differs by market. | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea): Higher in saturated fat than sunflower/canola/corn oil blends; frequent consumption adds more saturated fat to the diet.
consumer_note: Sugar: No ban anywhere; a conventional sweetener, though nutrition guidance recommends moderating total added-sugar intake. | Milk Solids (minimum 22%): No regulatory concerns; standard dairy ingredient, must be declared as an allergen. | Cocoa Butter: No regulatory concerns identified by leading food safety authorities. | Emulsifiers (442, 476): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Flavours: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea): Allowed everywhere; the choice of palm oil versus other vegetable oils is a manufacturer cost decision, not a regulatory requirement.
verified_sources: Sugar: WHO sugar intake guideline; general knowledge | Milk Solids (minimum 22%): General allergen labelling knowledge | Cocoa Butter: General knowledge; EU Cocoa and Chocolate Directive 2000/36/EC | Emulsifiers (442, 476): EU Regulation (EC) 1333/2008 Annex II (chocolate emulsifier limits); EFSA PGPR re-evaluation | Flavours: General flavouring-regulation knowledge | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea): General nutrition science; FSSAI has no oil-type mandate for snacks

barcode_source_note: Not found/verified in this session – barcodes vary by pack size and change over time; scan the physical package or check GS1 India / Open Food Facts to confirm.
india_ingredient_1: Carbonated water, sucrose, glucose, citric acid, taurine, sodium bicarbonate, magnesium carbonate, caffeine, niacinamide, calcium pantothenate, pyridoxine HCl (B6), vitamin B12, natural and artificial flavours, colour
india_ingredient_2: Same core global formula (carbonated water, sugars, taurine, caffeine, B vitamins), per Red Bull's standard worldwide recipe
india_ingredient_3: The base recipe is largely standardised worldwide; the meaningful difference is regulatory. India's FSSAI has historically classified energy drinks alongside carbonated beverages, which briefly capped caffeine at 145-200 ppm and led to a 2005 customs seizure of a Red Bull shipment in Chennai for exceeding that limit, whereas the EU has no such ppm cap and instead mandates a caffeine advisory label.
india_ingredient_4: Caffeine, taurine, glucuronolactone-related B-vitamin blend, colour
india_ingredient_5: None of the major 14 allergens typically declared; check label for facility cross-contact
india_ingredient_6: EU Regulation (EC) No 1169/2011 requires drinks with caffeine above 150mg/L to carry the statement 'High caffeine content. Not recommended for children or pregnant or breast-feeding women'; India has no dedicated energy drink caffeine standard under the FSS Act, historically applying carbonated-beverage caffeine limits instead.
india_ingredient_7: High caffeine intake can cause palpitations, anxiety and sleep disruption; the EU's mandatory advisory label gives consumers, including pregnant women, clearer guidance than a general nutrition panel.
india_ingredient_8: Red Bull India product page (redbull.com), Wikipedia 'Red Bull', Centre for Science and Environment (cseindia.org), FSSAI draft standards document
ingredient_explanation: Carbonated Water [Water / Carbonated Water]: Purified water, carbonated with CO2 for fizzy drinks. | Sucrose [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Citric Acid [Citric Acid (INS/E330)]: A natural acid (found in citrus fruit) used to adjust tartness and act as a preservative/acidity regulator. | Taurine [Taurine]: An amino-sulfonic acid naturally found in meat and fish, added to energy drinks as a functional ingredient. | Sodium Bicarbonate [B-Vitamins (Niacinamide, Calcium Pantothenate, Pyridoxine HCl/B6, Vitamin B12) & Sodium Bicarbonate/Magnesium Carbonate]: Added vitamins for fortification, and mineral salts (sodium bicarbonate, magnesium carbonate) used as acidity regulators/carbonation aids. | Caffeine [Caffeine]: A natural stimulant found in coffee, tea and guarana, added to energy drinks and colas for alertness effect. | Natural and Artificial Flavours [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Colour [Caramel Colour, Class IV / Sulphite Ammonia Caramel (150d/INS150d)]: A brown colourant made by heating sugar with ammonium and sulphite compounds; gives cola drinks their dark colour. | B Vitamins [Fortification Vitamins & Minerals (Iron, Zinc, Vitamin A/C/D/B-complex, Folic Acid)]: Micronutrients added to improve the nutritional profile of cereals and infant foods.

india_ingredient_3: Lay's in India is fried in palmolein (a processed palm oil fraction), while PepsiCo markets its US Lay's as cooked in 'heart-healthy' sunflower, corn or canola oil; PepsiCo India began trialling a sunflower-palmolein oil blend in 2024 after consumer criticism of the difference.
india_ingredient_4: None beyond seasoning salt in the classic variant; flavoured variants add additional seasonings and colours
india_ingredient_5: None of the major allergens in the classic salted variant; check flavoured variants for milk/gluten-based seasonings
india_ingredient_6: No FSSAI rule currently mandates a specific oil type for packaged snacks in India, unlike some public-health guidance in the US promoting unsaturated oils; the choice of palm oil in India is primarily a cost decision rather than a regulatory requirement.
india_ingredient_7: Palmolein is higher in saturated fat than sunflower, corn or canola oil, which contain more mono- and polyunsaturated fats associated with better cholesterol profiles when they replace saturated fats in the diet.
india_ingredient_8: Business Standard, Medindia (medindia.net), m9.news, PepsiCo statements reported May 2024

india_ingredient_1: Carbonated water, sugar (sucrose), caramel colour (150d), phosphoric acid, natural flavourings, caffeine
india_ingredient_2: Carbonated water, high fructose corn syrup, caramel colour, phosphoric acid, natural flavors, caffeine
india_ingredient_3: Coca-Cola sold in India (like most countries) is sweetened with cane sugar (sucrose), while the standard US formula uses high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS) as its primary sweetener; the US and Canada are the main markets where HFCS predominates.
india_ingredient_5: None of the major declared allergens
india_ingredient_6: No FSSAI rule mandates a specific sweetener type; HFCS use in the US reflects decades-old agricultural subsidy and cost structures rather than a food-safety distinction between the two sweeteners.
india_ingredient_7: Nutrition scientists generally consider cane sugar and HFCS nutritionally similar in calories and metabolic effect at equivalent sweetness; neither is a 'healthy' choice given the overall added-sugar content of regular soft drinks.
india_ingredient_8: Coca-Cola US official product page (coca-cola.com), Business Standard, Newsweek, CNN (July 2025 reporting on Coca-Cola's US cane-sugar variant)
ingredient_explanation: Carbonated Water [Water / Carbonated Water]: Purified water, carbonated with CO2 for fizzy drinks. | Sugar (Sucrose) [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Caramel Colour (150d) [Caramel Colour, Class IV / Sulphite Ammonia Caramel (150d/INS150d)]: A brown colourant made by heating sugar with ammonium and sulphite compounds; gives cola drinks their dark colour. | Phosphoric Acid [Phosphoric Acid (INS/E338)]: A mineral acid used to add tartness and tang to cola-type soft drinks. | Natural Flavourings [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Caffeine [Caffeine]: A natural stimulant found in coffee, tea and guarana, added to energy drinks and colas for alertness effect. | High Fructose Corn Syrup [High-Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS)]: A corn-starch derived liquid sweetener, mostly glucose and fructose.

banned_country: None identified in the 8 reviewed jurisdictions
restricted_country: Caramel Colour (150d): California (US) requires a cancer-warning label under Proposition 65 if the by-product 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI) exceeds a set threshold - a state-level, not federal, requirement | Caffeine: EU Regulation (EC) No 1169/2011 mandates a 'High caffeine content. Not recommended for children or pregnant or breast-feeding women' warning on drinks above 150mg/L caffeine (other than tea/coffee); India's FSSAI has no dedicated energy-drink caffeine standard and historically applied general carbonated-beverage limits
regulatory_status: Carbonated Water: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Sugar (Sucrose): Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Caramel Colour (150d): Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Phosphoric Acid: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Natural Flavourings: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions | Caffeine: Restricted/Warning-label required in at least one reviewed jurisdiction | High Fructose Corn Syrup: Allowed in all 8 reviewed jurisdictions
health_concern: Carbonated Water: None. | Sugar (Sucrose): High intake linked to obesity and dental caries; WHO recommends limiting free sugars. | Caramel Colour (150d): 4-MEI, a trace by-product of Class IV caramel colour manufacture, has been classified as a possible carcinogen by some assessments at high doses in animal studies; typical dietary exposure is well below levels of concern per JECFA/EFSA. | Phosphoric Acid: Some observational research links high cola/phosphoric-acid intake to lower bone density, though causation is unproven; not a basis for regulatory restriction. | Natural Flavourings: None identified; standard flavouring practice, though labelling terminology (natural vs nature-identical vs artificial) differs by market. | Caffeine: High caffeine intake can cause palpitations, anxiety and sleep disruption, particularly relevant for children, pregnant women and caffeine-sensitive individuals. | High Fructose Corn Syrup: Nutritionally similar to sucrose per calorie; not shown to be uniquely harmful, but is a marker of a highly processed product.
consumer_note: Carbonated Water: No regulatory concerns. | Sugar (Sucrose): No ban anywhere; a conventional sweetener, though nutrition guidance recommends moderating total added-sugar intake. | Caramel Colour (150d): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities at normal consumption levels; California uniquely requires a warning label above a specific 4-MEI threshold. | Phosphoric Acid: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Natural Flavourings: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Caffeine: Allowed in India but the EU/UK require a specific high-caffeine warning label that India does not mandate for energy drinks. | High Fructose Corn Syrup: Not banned or restricted anywhere; its scarcity outside the US mainly reflects manufacturer recipe/cost choices, not regulation.

india_ingredient_1: Refined wheat flour, sugar, fractionated palm oil (palmolein), cocoa solids, inverted sugar syrup, raising agents, salt, emulsifiers, artificial flavouring; no high-fructose corn syrup
india_ingredient_2: Enriched wheat flour, sugar, palm and/or canola oil, cocoa (processed with alkali), high-fructose corn syrup, leavening agents, salt, soy lecithin, chocolate, artificial flavor
india_ingredient_3: A viral claim about Oreo's ingredients was fact-checked and confirmed that Oreo biscuits sold in India do not list high-fructose corn syrup as an ingredient, unlike the standard US formulation, which does.
india_ingredient_4: Fractionated palm oil, inverted sugar syrup (India); high-fructose corn syrup, soy lecithin (US)
india_ingredient_5: Wheat, Gluten, Soy (US variant, via soy lecithin); may contain milk (cross-contact, both variants)
india_ingredient_6: No specific FSSAI rule bans HFCS; its absence in the India formulation is a manufacturer recipe choice rather than a regulatory mandate, similar to most European Oreo formulations which also exclude HFCS.
india_ingredient_7: HFCS and sugar are considered nutritionally comparable per calorie; the more relevant health point for either version is the overall sugar and refined-flour content typical of a sandwich biscuit.
india_ingredient_8: Fact Crescendo fact-check (English.factcrescendo.com, 2026), Oreo ingredient list via Wikipedia 'Oreo' and Chef's Resource
ingredient_explanation: Refined Wheat Flour [Wheat Flour / Maida (refined wheat flour)]: Milled wheat grain, refined (maida) or whole; provides starch and gluten structure. | Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Fractionated Palm Oil (Palmolein) [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Cocoa Solids [Cocoa Solids / Cocoa Mass / Cocoa Butter]: Components derived from roasted cocoa beans. | Raising Agents [B-Vitamins (Niacinamide, Calcium Pantothenate, Pyridoxine HCl/B6, Vitamin B12) & Sodium Bicarbonate/Magnesium Carbonate]: Added vitamins for fortification, and mineral salts (sodium bicarbonate, magnesium carbonate) used as acidity regulators/carbonation aids. | Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Emulsifiers [Ammonium Phosphatides (E442/INS442) & Polyglycerol Polyricinoleate / PGPR (E476/INS476)]: Emulsifiers that reduce the amount of cocoa butter needed and improve chocolate's flow/viscosity during moulding. | Artificial Flavouring [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Palm and/or Canola Oil [Vegetable Oil (Corn/Soybean/Canola/Sunflower blend)]: A blend of seed oils used as a frying/cooking medium. | High-Fructose Corn Syrup [High-Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS)]: A corn-starch derived liquid sweetener, mostly glucose and fructose. | Soy Lecithin [Lecithin (Soy/Sunflower) - INS/E322]: A natural fat-derived emulsifier that helps oil and water-based ingredients blend smoothly.

health_concern: Refined Wheat Flour: Contains gluten; relevant only for those with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. | Sugar: High intake linked to obesity and dental caries; WHO recommends limiting free sugars. | Fractionated Palm Oil (Palmolein): Higher in saturated fat than sunflower/canola/corn oil blends; frequent consumption adds more saturated fat to the diet. | Cocoa Solids: None beyond typical chocolate sugar/fat content. | Raising Agents: None identified at fortification levels. | Salt: High sodium intake is linked to elevated blood pressure risk; WHO recommends under 5g salt/day. | Emulsifiers: Reviewed as safe within EFSA's established Acceptable Daily Intake for PGPR; no major health concerns identified for either substance at typical chocolate-use levels. | Artificial Flavouring: None identified; standard flavouring practice, though labelling terminology (natural vs nature-identical vs artificial) differs by market. | Palm and/or Canola Oil: Higher in unsaturated fat than palm oil; generally considered a favourable substitute for saturated fats. | High-Fructose Corn Syrup: Nutritionally similar to sucrose per calorie; not shown to be uniquely harmful, but is a marker of a highly processed product. | Soy Lecithin: Soy lecithin is a potential allergen trace for soy-allergic individuals, though highly refined lecithin contains negligible protein.

india_ingredient_1: Milled corn, sugar, salt, malt flavouring, vitamins and minerals (iron, niacin, vitamin B6, B2, B1, folic acid, vitamin D, B12); salt content approx. 1.93g per 100g

india_ingredient_3: A 2016 international breakfast cereal survey found Kellogg's Corn Flakes sold in India had the highest salt content among the markets tested (1.93g/100g), around 46% more than the same cereal in Argentina/Brazil and notably higher than the UK's reformulated low-salt version (1.13g/100g).

india_ingredient_5: May contain milk, may contain nuts (cross-contact); gluten-free corn base but shared-line risk
india_ingredient_6: The UK has pursued a voluntary national salt-reduction programme with food industry salt targets since the 2000s; India's FSSAI has no equivalent mandatory salt-reduction target for breakfast cereals.
india_ingredient_7: Regularly consuming higher-sodium packaged foods contributes to overall dietary sodium intake, a recognised factor in elevated blood pressure risk; the World Health Organization recommends under 5g of salt per day from all sources.
india_ingredient_8: FoodIngredientsFirst.com (2016 global breakfast cereal salt/sugar survey), Kellogg's official product listings (India and Europe)

india_ingredient_1: Water, tomato paste (24-26%), sugar, dextrose, brewed vinegar, iodized salt, thickener (INS 1422, INS 415), mixed spices, yeast extract, onion powder, garlic powder, sweetener (INS 960), capsicum oleoresin
india_ingredient_2: Tomato concentrate from red ripe tomatoes, distilled vinegar, high-fructose corn syrup, corn syrup, salt, spice, onion powder, natural flavoring
india_ingredient_3: Heinz India's ketchup is sweetened with sugar and dextrose (no high-fructose corn syrup) and includes a small-quantity approved thickener and steviol-glycoside sweetener blend not used in the standard US formulation, which instead relies on HFCS and corn syrup as its main sweeteners.
india_ingredient_4: Thickener INS 1422 (modified starch), INS 415 (xanthan gum), sweetener INS 960 (steviol glycosides) in the India formulation; high-fructose corn syrup in the US formulation
india_ingredient_5: None of the major declared allergens in either variant; check for celery in some regional Heinz formulations (UK/EU)
india_ingredient_6: FSSAI's Indian Standard IS:3882 for tomato ketchup permits specific classes of thickener and preservative up to 750ppm; there is no maximum-added-sugar limit specified by FSSAI or BIS for ketchup, unlike stricter added-sugar labelling pushes underway in the US and EU.
india_ingredient_7: Ketchup is a concentrated source of added sugar and salt relative to its serving size in most markets; consumers managing sodium or sugar intake should check the nutrition panel rather than assume either version is 'healthier'.
india_ingredient_8: Heinz India retail listings (Zepto, Amazon.in, Flipkart), Heinz US official product page (heinz.com), Consumer Voice comparative test (consumer-voice.org)
ingredient_explanation: Water [Water / Carbonated Water]: Purified water, carbonated with CO2 for fizzy drinks. | Tomato Paste (24-26%) [Tomato Paste/Concentrate & Tomato Powder]: Concentrated, cooked-down tomato used as the flavour base of ketchup and seasonings. | Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Dextrose [Dextrose (Glucose)]: A simple sugar chemically identical to glucose. | Brewed Vinegar [Vinegar (Brewed/Distilled)]: An acid produced by fermenting alcohol into acetic acid, used for tang and preservation. | Iodized Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Thickener (INS 1422, INS 415) [Xanthan Gum (INS/E415)]: A thickener produced by fermenting sugar with bacteria, giving sauces a smooth, stable texture. | Mixed Spices [Onion Powder / Garlic Powder / Spices]: Dried, ground allium vegetables and spice blends for savoury flavour. | Yeast Extract [Yeast Extract]: A savoury, umami-rich extract from yeast, used as a natural alternative flavour-enhancer to isolated MSG. | Sweetener (INS 960) [Steviol Glycosides (INS/E960, Stevia extract)]: A zero-calorie sweetener extracted from the stevia leaf, roughly 200-300x sweeter than sugar. | Capsicum Oleoresin [Carotenes / Beta-apo-carotenal - INS/E160a, E160e, and Colour 160c (paprika/capsanthin oleoresin)]: Naturally-occurring yellow-orange-red pigments related to beta-carotene, often sourced from carrots, palm oil, or paprika. | High-Fructose Corn Syrup [High-Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS)]: A corn-starch derived liquid sweetener, mostly glucose and fructose. | Natural Flavoring [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends.

other_country_ingredient_3: Natural Beef Flavor (Wheat and Milk Derivatives)
key_differences: McDonald's has confirmed to Indian media that its French Fries in India are cooked without any 'Natural Beef Flavor' or animal-derived extract, making them vegetarian; the US version uses a beef-flavoured oil blend containing hydrolysed wheat and milk derivatives (added after the chain switched from beef tallow to vegetable oil in 1990) to replicate the original taste.
notable_additives: Sodium Acid Pyrophosphate, Dimethylpolysiloxane, TBHQ (US version only, per supplier-level ingredient statement)
allergens: Wheat, Milk (US version, via Natural Beef Flavor); India version has no declared beef/animal allergen
regulatory_difference: No FSSAI rule mandates flavour disclosure beyond standard allergen labelling; the US formulation is permitted under US 'natural flavor' rules that do not require McDonald's to state whether beef-derived material is present, per FDA labelling rules.
health_note: The India version is suitable for vegetarians and vegans; the US version is not vegetarian-friendly due to wheat and milk derivatives in its beef flavouring, an important distinction for those with dairy or gluten sensitivities or religious dietary restrictions.
source: DailyO fact-check (dailyo.in, April 2023), The Takeout, Chef's Resource, McDonald's USA official ingredient statement, VRG (Vegetarian Resource Group) January 2025 update

other_country_ingredient_8: Colors (Red 40 Lake, Yellow 5 Lake, Blue 2 Lake, Yellow 6 Lake, Blue 1 Lake, Yellow 6, Red 40, Yellow 5, Blue 1)
key_differences: Skittles sold in India (imported, EU-style formulation) use a mixed palette including several natural/nature-derived colours (beetroot red E162, anthocyanins E163, carotenes E160a, curcumin E100) alongside some synthetic dyes; the US formulation relies almost entirely on synthetic azo/triphenylmethane dyes (Red 40, Yellow 5, Yellow 6, Blue 1) with no natural colour sources.
notable_additives: Titanium dioxide (historically used in US formulation, phased out by mid-2025); synthetic Lake dyes (US); natural colour sources (India/EU import)
allergens: None of the major 14 allergens declared in either version; manufactured on lines that may process milk
regulatory_difference: EU/UK regulations require a warning label ('may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children') on foods containing certain synthetic dyes including Red 40, Yellow 5 and Yellow 6; the US FDA has not mandated an equivalent warning, and FSSAI permits most of these dyes without a comparable label requirement.
health_note: Some studies have linked certain synthetic azo dyes to hyperactivity in sensitive children, prompting the EU/UK warning-label requirement; the natural colour blend used in the India/EU-import version avoids these specific dyes, though nutritionally the product remains a high-sugar confection regardless of colour source.
source: Cococart India product listing (cococart.in), Skittles.com official US ingredient panel, Imbarex color/ingredient analyses, FSSAI Food Additives Regulations 2011
ingredient_explanation: Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Palm Fat [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Acids (Citric Acid, Malic Acid) [Citric Acid (INS/E330)]: A natural acid (found in citrus fruit) used to adjust tartness and act as a preservative/acidity regulator. | Dextrin [Dextrin]: A starch breakdown product used to control texture and adhesion (e.g., in chocolate shells). | Maltodextrin [Maltodextrin]: A mildly-sweet starch-derived carbohydrate used as a bulking/carrier agent. | Flavourings [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Modified Starch [Starch / Corn Starch / Modified Starch (INS 1422 etc.)]: A carbohydrate thickener, usually derived from corn, potato, or tapioca; 'modified' starches are chemically or physically altered for stability. | Colours (E162, E163, E170, E160a, E100, E132, E133) [Synthetic Azo/Triarylmethane Dyes - Red 40 (Allura Red, E129/INS129), Yellow 5 (Tartrazine, E102/INS102), Yellow 6 (Sunset Yellow FCF, E110/INS110), Blue 1 (Brilliant Blue FCF, E133/INS133), Blue 2 (Indigotine, E132/INS132), incl. their aluminium 'lake' forms]: Petroleum-derived synthetic colourants used to give bright, uniform reds/yellows/blues. | Acidity Regulator (Trisodium Citrate) [Sodium Citrate (INS/E331)]: The sodium salt of citric acid, used to buffer acidity and add a mild tart-salty note. | Glazing Agent (Carnauba Wax) [Carnauba Wax (INS/E903)]: A hard wax from carnauba palm leaves, used to give confectionery a shiny coating.

other_country_ingredient_2: Vegetable Oil (Corn, Cottonseed, Canola, High Oleic Soybean, and/or Sunflower)
key_differences: Pringles sold in India (Kellogg India Pvt. Ltd.) are fried exclusively in palm oil and include an added acidity regulator (citric acid, INS 330) not listed in the US version; the US formulation uses a multi-oil blend (corn, cottonseed, canola, soybean, sunflower) with no palm oil, and adds rice flour and wheat starch, both absent from the India ingredient list.
notable_additives: Emulsifier INS 471/Mono- and diglycerides (both versions, different naming); Acidity Regulator INS 330 (India only)
allergens: Wheat (US version, via wheat starch); India version list does not declare wheat
regulatory_difference: No FSSAI rule mandates disclosure of a specific frying oil for extruded snacks; the choice of 100% palm oil in the India formulation versus a blended-oil approach in the US is a manufacturer cost and sourcing decision rather than a regulatory requirement.
health_note: Palm oil is comparatively higher in saturated fat than the corn/canola/sunflower oil blend used in the US version; frequent consumption of palm-oil-fried snacks contributes more saturated fat to the diet than oils richer in unsaturated fats.
source: Open Food Facts product pages (Kellogg India Pvt. Ltd. barcode 8886467122392; US barcode listing), Pringles.com official US ingredient panel
ingredient_explanation: Dried Potato (48%) [Potato]: A starchy root vegetable, the base ingredient of chips/fries. | Edible Vegetable Oil (Palm Oil) [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Corn Flour [Wheat Flour / Maida (refined wheat flour)]: Milled wheat grain, refined (maida) or whole; provides starch and gluten structure. | Starch [Starch / Corn Starch / Modified Starch (INS 1422 etc.)]: A carbohydrate thickener, usually derived from corn, potato, or tapioca; 'modified' starches are chemically or physically altered for stability. | Emulsifier (INS 471) [Mono- and Diglycerides of Fatty Acids (INS 471)]: Common emulsifiers derived from vegetable or animal fats, used to keep fat and water phases blended. | Maltodextrin [Maltodextrin]: A mildly-sweet starch-derived carbohydrate used as a bulking/carrier agent. | Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Acidity Regulator (INS 330) [Citric Acid (INS/E330)]: A natural acid (found in citrus fruit) used to adjust tartness and act as a preservative/acidity regulator. | Vegetable Oil (Corn, Cottonseed, Canola, High Oleic Soybean, and/or Sunflower) [Vegetable Oil (Corn/Soybean/Canola/Sunflower blend)]: A blend of seed oils used as a frying/cooking medium. | Wheat Starch [Toasted Wheat Germ / Wheat Starch]: Nutrient-dense wheat embryo (germ) or its starch fraction, used for texture and toasted flavour in wafer-based confectionery.

key_differences: A comparison publicised in February 2026 by consumer advocate Revant Himatsingka ('Food Pharmer') found KitKat sold in India contains 16.1% milk solids, 4.5% cocoa solids and 28.9g of added sugar, versus 20% milk solids, 22% cocoa solids and 21.7g of added sugar in the Australian version, indicating the India bar is formulated with substantially less milk and cocoa and more added sugar per bar.
notable_additives: Emulsifiers (both versions); higher added-sugar loading (India version)
allergens: Milk, Wheat, may contain Soy, Nuts (both versions, standard KitKat allergen declaration)
regulatory_difference: No FSSAI rule sets a minimum cocoa-solids or milk-solids threshold for 'chocolate wafer' products of this type sold in India, unlike Australia's food standards which set compositional minimums for products labelled as milk chocolate.
health_note: A chocolate product with lower cocoa and milk-solids content and higher added sugar shifts more of each bar's calories toward refined sugar rather than cocoa and dairy nutrients; frequent consumption of the higher-sugar version contributes more to overall added-sugar intake.
source: Storyboard18 (Feb 11, 2026), Revant Himatsingka / 'Food Pharmer' public post (X/LinkedIn, February 2026), 'Label Padhega India' consumer initiative
ingredient_explanation: Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Wheat Flour [Wheat Flour / Maida (refined wheat flour)]: Milled wheat grain, refined (maida) or whole; provides starch and gluten structure. | Milk Solids (16.1%) [Milk Solids / Milk Powder]: Dried milk components (protein, lactose, fat) used to add dairy richness. | Vegetable Fat [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Cocoa Solids (4.5%) [Cocoa Solids / Cocoa Mass / Cocoa Butter]: Components derived from roasted cocoa beans. | Emulsifiers [Ammonium Phosphatides (E442/INS442) & Polyglycerol Polyricinoleate / PGPR (E476/INS476)]: Emulsifiers that reduce the amount of cocoa butter needed and improve chocolate's flow/viscosity during moulding. | Raising Agent [B-Vitamins (Niacinamide, Calcium Pantothenate, Pyridoxine HCl/B6, Vitamin B12) & Sodium Bicarbonate/Magnesium Carbonate]: Added vitamins for fortification, and mineral salts (sodium bicarbonate, magnesium carbonate) used as acidity regulators/carbonation aids. | Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Flavouring [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends.

key_differences: Domino's UK states it uses 100% full-fat mozzarella made from milk with comparatively stricter EU/UK compositional rules limiting non-dairy fillers; multiple food-industry reviews report that Domino's cheese blends in cost-sensitive markets including India contain a higher proportion of vegetable oil and starch-based fillers alongside mozzarella to reduce cost, unlike the UK's single-ingredient full-fat mozzarella claim.
notable_additives: Modified starch and anti-caking/stabiliser blends used to reduce pure-cheese content in cost-sensitive markets
regulatory_difference: No FSSAI standard mandates a minimum real-cheese percentage for 'pizza cheese' or processed cheese analogue products used as pizza toppings, unlike UK/EU compositional standards for foods labelled as 'cheese,' which restrict non-dairy additions.
health_note: A cheese blend with added vegetable oil and starch has a different fat and protein profile than 100% dairy mozzarella; consumers seeking higher dairy-protein content should be aware that 'cheese' toppings on fast-food pizza can vary significantly in actual cheese percentage between markets.
source: Cheese Scientist (cheesescientist.com, Feb 2025), Domino's UK public statements on mozzarella sourcing, Marketing Scoop cheese-blend analysis
ingredient_explanation: Mozzarella Cheese (Milk, Salt, Cultures, Enzymes) [Cheese (Mozzarella/Cheddar/Romano) & Cheese Powder]: Dairy-derived cheese, either as whole cheese or as a dried, powdered flavouring form. | Vegetable Oil [Vegetable Oil (Corn/Soybean/Canola/Sunflower blend)]: A blend of seed oils used as a frying/cooking medium. | Modified Starch [Starch / Corn Starch / Modified Starch (INS 1422 etc.)]: A carbohydrate thickener, usually derived from corn, potato, or tapioca; 'modified' starches are chemically or physically altered for stability. | Anti-caking Agent [Anticaking Agent, Silicon Dioxide (INS/E551)]: A fine mineral powder that keeps powdered seasonings from clumping.

india_ingredient_10: Sugar, Salt, Colours E100 (Curcumin), E133 (Brilliant Blue), E160a, E160e
other_country_ingredient_1: Milk Chocolate (Sugar, Cocoa Butter, Chocolate, Skim Milk, Milk Fat, Lactose, Soy Lecithin, Salt, Artificial Flavor)
other_country_ingredient_6: Coloring (Blue 1 Lake, Red 40 Lake, Yellow 6, Yellow 5, Blue 1, Yellow 6 Lake, Yellow 5 Lake, Blue 2 Lake, Blue 2)
key_differences: M&M's launched in India in 2017 (Mars India). The India version's colour system (per foodnetindia's label breakdown) includes curcumin (E100, a natural turmeric-derived yellow) and beta-carotene-type colours (E160a, E160e) alongside brilliant blue (E133), giving it a partly natural colour base. The US version relies entirely on synthetic Lake dyes (Red 40, Yellow 5, Yellow 6, Blue 1/2 and their lake forms) with no natural colour sources declared.
notable_additives: Curcumin, natural carotenoid colours (India); synthetic Lake dyes (US)
allergens: Milk, Soy/Coconut derivatives possible (India); Milk, Soy (US)
regulatory_difference: FSSAI permits both natural and synthetic colours under the Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011, up to specified limits; there is no FSSAI mandate favouring natural colours, so the partial natural-colour formulation in India reflects Mars' own recipe choice for this market rather than a regulatory requirement.
health_note: Synthetic azo dyes such as those used in the US formulation have been the subject of research (including UK Food Standards Agency-funded studies) linking them to hyperactivity in sensitive children; the India formulation's partial use of natural colourants like curcumin reduces exposure to some (but not all) of these specific dyes.
source: Foodnetindia.in M&M's ingredient review, ConfectioneryNews.com (August 2017, Mars India launch), Mars official M&M'S US ingredient panel
ingredient_explanation: Cocoa Mass [Cocoa Solids / Cocoa Mass / Cocoa Butter]: Components derived from roasted cocoa beans. | Full Cream Milk Powder [Milk Solids / Milk Powder]: Dried milk components (protein, lactose, fat) used to add dairy richness. | Coconut Oil [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Natural Vanilla Extract [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Starch [Starch / Corn Starch / Modified Starch (INS 1422 etc.)]: A carbohydrate thickener, usually derived from corn, potato, or tapioca; 'modified' starches are chemically or physically altered for stability. | Dextrin [Dextrin]: A starch breakdown product used to control texture and adhesion (e.g., in chocolate shells). | Stabiliser: Gum Arabic (E414) [Gum Arabic / Acacia Gum (E414/INS414)]: A natural gum from acacia tree sap, used to stabilise texture and coatings. | Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Colours E100 (Curcumin) [Curcumin / INS or E100 (Turmeric extract)]: A yellow-orange pigment extracted from turmeric root. | E133 (Brilliant Blue) [Synthetic Azo/Triarylmethane Dyes - Red 40 (Allura Red, E129/INS129), Yellow 5 (Tartrazine, E102/INS102), Yellow 6 (Sunset Yellow FCF, E110/INS110), Blue 1 (Brilliant Blue FCF, E133/INS133), Blue 2 (Indigotine, E132/INS132), incl. their aluminium 'lake' forms]: Petroleum-derived synthetic colourants used to give bright, uniform reds/yellows/blues. | E160a [Carotenes / Beta-apo-carotenal - INS/E160a, E160e, and Colour 160c (paprika/capsanthin oleoresin)]: Naturally-occurring yellow-orange-red pigments related to beta-carotene, often sourced from carrots, palm oil, or paprika. | Lactose [Cheese (Mozzarella/Cheddar/Romano) & Cheese Powder]: Dairy-derived cheese, either as whole cheese or as a dried, powdered flavouring form. | Soy Lecithin [Lecithin (Soy/Sunflower) - INS/E322]: A natural fat-derived emulsifier that helps oil and water-based ingredients blend smoothly.

health_concern: Cocoa Mass: None beyond typical chocolate sugar/fat content. | Full Cream Milk Powder: Milk is a major declared allergen; relevant for those with dairy allergy or lactose intolerance. | Coconut Oil: Higher in saturated fat than sunflower/canola/corn oil blends; frequent consumption adds more saturated fat to the diet. | Natural Vanilla Extract: None identified; standard flavouring practice, though labelling terminology (natural vs nature-identical vs artificial) differs by market. | Starch: None identified; long history of safe use. | Dextrin: None identified. | Stabiliser: Gum Arabic (E414): None identified; long safe-use history. | Sugar: High intake linked to obesity and dental caries; WHO recommends limiting free sugars. | Salt: High sodium intake is linked to elevated blood pressure risk; WHO recommends under 5g salt/day. | Colours E100 (Curcumin): No safety concerns identified; a long-used spice-derived colour. | E133 (Brilliant Blue): Some studies (e.g., the 2007 Southampton study funded by the UK FSA) have associated these dyes with hyperactivity in sensitive children, prompting the EU/UK warning label; evidence remains debated and the FDA has not found cause for a US ban. | E160a: No safety concerns identified. | Lactose: Blends using added starch/vegetable oil alongside real cheese have a different fat/protein profile than 100% dairy cheese. | Soy Lecithin: Soy lecithin is a potential allergen trace for soy-allergic individuals, though highly refined lecithin contains negligible protein.
consumer_note: Cocoa Mass: No regulatory concerns identified by leading food safety authorities. | Full Cream Milk Powder: No regulatory concerns; standard dairy ingredient, must be declared as an allergen. | Coconut Oil: Allowed everywhere; the choice of palm oil versus other vegetable oils is a manufacturer cost decision, not a regulatory requirement. | Natural Vanilla Extract: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Starch: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Dextrin: No regulatory concerns identified. | Stabiliser: Gum Arabic (E414): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Sugar: No ban anywhere; a conventional sweetener, though nutrition guidance recommends moderating total added-sugar intake. | Salt: Allowed in India but restricted in some countries' voluntary reformulation programmes due to concerns over excessive sodium consumption. | Colours E100 (Curcumin): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | E133 (Brilliant Blue): Allowed in India and most of the world, but the EU/UK require a child-hyperactivity warning label on food containing these dyes - a label India does not currently mandate. | E160a: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Lactose: No safety concern; a labelling-transparency and recipe-composition difference rather than a regulatory ban. | Soy Lecithin: No major regulatory concerns; must be declared if soy-derived, for allergen labelling.

key_differences: Knorr products sold in India (e.g., Knorr All Purpose Seasoning, per its Amazon.in/HUL listing) explicitly declare a combination of MSG, I+G (disodium inosinate and guanylate), and HVP as core flavour-enhancing ingredients. Knorr's standard UK stock cube range is marketed and labelled as having 'no added MSG,' relying instead on yeast extract as its primary umami source, per Unilever's own UK product statements.
notable_additives: MSG, I+G, HVP (India); Yeast extract as MSG substitute (UK)
allergens: Soy, Wheat possible via HVP (India); Celery, Milk possible depending on UK SKU
regulatory_difference: FSSAI permits MSG (INS 621) as a flavour enhancer in seasonings without a specific upper limit beyond Good Manufacturing Practice; the UK/EU 'no added MSG' claim reflects Unilever's own marketing/formulation choice for that market rather than a regulatory prohibition on MSG.
health_note: MSG is classified as Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) by international food safety bodies; a small subset of MSG-sensitive individuals report symptoms like headache or flushing after consuming products with concentrated added MSG, which the yeast-extract-based UK formulation would not trigger in the same way.
source: Amazon.in Knorr All Purpose Seasoning product listing (Hindustan Unilever Ltd.), Knorr.com UK/US official FAQ, Wellnd.com Knorr ingredient breakdown
ingredient_explanation: Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Corn Starch [Starch / Corn Starch / Modified Starch (INS 1422 etc.)]: A carbohydrate thickener, usually derived from corn, potato, or tapioca; 'modified' starches are chemically or physically altered for stability. | Monosodium Glutamate (MSG) [Monosodium Glutamate (MSG) / INS or E621]: The sodium salt of glutamic acid, a naturally-occurring amino acid; delivers the savoury 'umami' taste. | Disodium Inosinate and Disodium Guanylate (I+G) [Disodium Inosinate & Disodium Guanylate (I+G, INS 631/627)]: Nucleotide flavour enhancers, often used together with MSG to intensify umami/savoury taste. | Hydrolyzed Vegetable Protein (HVP) [Hydrolysed Vegetable Protein (HVP)]: Plant protein (often soy, wheat, or groundnut) broken down by acid/enzymes to release natural glutamate for savoury flavour, without adding isolated MSG. | Flavourings [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Spices [Onion Powder / Garlic Powder / Spices]: Dried, ground allium vegetables and spice blends for savoury flavour. | Acidity Regulators [Citric Acid (INS/E330)]: A natural acid (found in citrus fruit) used to adjust tartness and act as a preservative/acidity regulator. | Vegetable Fats (Palm, Shea, Sal) [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Yeast Extract [Yeast Extract]: A savoury, umami-rich extract from yeast, used as a natural alternative flavour-enhancer to isolated MSG.

key_differences: Cadbury Bournville's India 'Rich Cocoa 70%' variant (per Cococart India/Mondelez India Foods label) is formulated with a combined cocoa solids-plus-cocoa-butter content of roughly 73% and lists no added palm oil, using cocoa butter as its primary fat. The UK 'Classic' Bournville has a lower minimum cocoa solids threshold (36%) and explicitly adds palm oil as a vegetable fat alongside cocoa butter, a permitted substitution under UK/EU chocolate rules (up to 5% non-cocoa vegetable fat).
notable_additives: Emulsifiers 442/E476 (both); Palm oil specifically (UK Classic only)
allergens: Milk (both); may contain nuts, soy (both, cross-contact)
regulatory_difference: EU/UK chocolate standards (Directive 2000/36/EC) permit up to 5% non-cocoa-butter vegetable fat substitution in chocolate labelled 'dark' or 'plain,' which the UK Classic Bournville uses; India's Bournville Rich Cocoa 70% variant does not use this permitted substitution, relying on cocoa butter alone as its fat source.
health_note: Chocolate with a higher proportion of cocoa solids and cocoa butter (as in India's Rich Cocoa 70% variant) generally has more of the flavanol compounds associated with dark chocolate's antioxidant properties compared to a lower-cocoa formulation cut with added palm oil and higher sugar, as in the UK Classic Bournville.
source: Cococart India product listing (Cadbury Bournville Rich Cocoa 70%, Mondelez India Foods), British Corner Shop UK Bournville Classic listing, Wikipedia 'Bournville (chocolate)'

key_differences: India's Doritos Nacho Cheese (per iShopIndian retail listing) is fried in palmolein (a palm oil fraction) and uses a generic 'cheese powder' with natural colour 160c (a carotenoid) rather than named cheddar/romano cheese. The US version uses a corn/soybean/sunflower oil blend (no palm oil), names cheddar and romano cheese explicitly, adds MSG directly, and colours the chip with a synthetic dye blend (Yellow 6, Yellow 5, Red 40).
allergens: Milk, Wheat (India, explicitly declared); Milk, Soy possible (US)
regulatory_difference: FSSAI does not mandate a specific frying oil or colour source for extruded corn snacks; the choice of palmolein and a natural carotenoid colour in India versus a multi-oil blend and synthetic dyes in the US reflects PepsiCo's separate regional formulation decisions rather than a regulatory requirement in either market.
health_note: Palmolein is higher in saturated fat than the corn/soybean/sunflower blend used in the US version; the US version's synthetic dye blend (Yellow 6, Yellow 5, Red 40) has been linked in some research to hyperactivity in sensitive children, an exposure the India version's natural colourant avoids.
source: iShopIndian.com Doritos Nacho Cheese (India) product listing, Wikipedia 'Doritos' (US ingredient panel), Doritos.com/Pop'N Flavors US ingredient sourcing
ingredient_explanation: Corn [Potato]: A starchy root vegetable, the base ingredient of chips/fries. | Edible Vegetable Oil (Palmolein) [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Seasoning: Milk Solids [Milk Solids / Milk Powder]: Dried milk components (protein, lactose, fat) used to add dairy richness. | Refined Wheat Flour (Maida) [Wheat Flour / Maida (refined wheat flour)]: Milled wheat grain, refined (maida) or whole; provides starch and gluten structure. | Spices and Condiments [Onion Powder / Garlic Powder / Spices]: Dried, ground allium vegetables and spice blends for savoury flavour. | Iodised Salt [Salt (Sodium Chloride)]: Common table salt; iodised salt has added potassium iodate. | Acidity Regulator (330) [Citric Acid (INS/E330)]: A natural acid (found in citrus fruit) used to adjust tartness and act as a preservative/acidity regulator. | Cheese Powder [Cheese (Mozzarella/Cheddar/Romano) & Cheese Powder]: Dairy-derived cheese, either as whole cheese or as a dried, powdered flavouring form. | Tomato Powder [Tomato Paste/Concentrate & Tomato Powder]: Concentrated, cooked-down tomato used as the flavour base of ketchup and seasonings. | Anticaking Agent (551) [Anticaking Agent, Silicon Dioxide (INS/E551)]: A fine mineral powder that keeps powdered seasonings from clumping. | Colour (160c) [Carotenes / Beta-apo-carotenal - INS/E160a, E160e, and Colour 160c (paprika/capsanthin oleoresin)]: Naturally-occurring yellow-orange-red pigments related to beta-carotene, often sourced from carrots, palm oil, or paprika. | Vegetable Oil (Corn, Soybean, and/or Sunflower Oil) [Vegetable Oil (Corn/Soybean/Canola/Sunflower blend)]: A blend of seed oils used as a frying/cooking medium. | Maltodextrin [Maltodextrin]: A mildly-sweet starch-derived carbohydrate used as a bulking/carrier agent. | Monosodium Glutamate [Monosodium Glutamate (MSG) / INS or E621]: The sodium salt of glutamic acid, a naturally-occurring amino acid; delivers the savoury 'umami' taste. | Partially Hydrogenated Soybean and Cottonseed Oil [Partially Hydrogenated Soybean/Cottonseed Oil (industrial trans fat source)]: Vegetable oil chemically hardened via partial hydrogenation, historically used for shelf-stable texture. | Disodium Phosphate [Sodium Acid Pyrophosphate (INS/E450)]: A phosphate salt used to keep cut potatoes/fries from discolouring and help even browning. | Natural and Artificial Flavor [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends. | Artificial Color (Yellow 6, Yellow 5, Red 40) [Synthetic Azo/Triarylmethane Dyes - Red 40 (Allura Red, E129/INS129), Yellow 5 (Tartrazine, E102/INS102), Yellow 6 (Sunset Yellow FCF, E110/INS110), Blue 1 (Brilliant Blue FCF, E133/INS133), Blue 2 (Indigotine, E132/INS132), incl. their aluminium 'lake' forms]: Petroleum-derived synthetic colourants used to give bright, uniform reds/yellows/blues. | Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Disodium Inosinate [Disodium Inosinate & Disodium Guanylate (I+G, INS 631/627)]: Nucleotide flavour enhancers, often used together with MSG to intensify umami/savoury taste.

banned_country: Partially Hydrogenated Soybean and Cottonseed Oil: United States (FDA revoked GRAS status for partially hydrogenated oils in 2015, compliance required from 2018, effectively removing them from the food supply)
restricted_country: Cheese Powder: EU/UK compositional standards for products labelled 'cheese' restrict non-dairy fillers more tightly than India's standards for 'pizza cheese'/processed cheese analogues | Anticaking Agent (551): EFSA re-evaluated E551 in 2018 and requested more data on nanoparticle content, without withdrawing approval | Partially Hydrogenated Soybean and Cottonseed Oil: India (FSSAI caps industrial trans-fatty acids in oils/fats at 2% by weight, effective January 2022); EU/UK/Canada/Australia/Singapore all cap industrial trans fat at 2g/100g fat under WHO-aligned rules | Disodium Phosphate: EU sets maximum phosphate use levels across food categories (Regulation (EC) 1333/2008); high cumulative phosphate intake from multiple processed-food sources is a general dietary consideration, especially for people with kidney disease | Artificial Color (Yellow 6, Yellow 5, Red 40): EU and UK: permitted, but Regulation (EC) 1333/2008 requires a mandatory warning - 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children' - on any product containing Yellow 5, Yellow 6, Red 40 (and 3 other 'Southampton Six' dyes); Norway and Austria voluntarily/historically restrict some of these further

health_concern: Corn: No regulatory concern; a conventional food. | Edible Vegetable Oil (Palmolein): Higher in saturated fat than sunflower/canola/corn oil blends; frequent consumption adds more saturated fat to the diet. | Seasoning: Milk Solids: Milk is a major declared allergen; relevant for those with dairy allergy or lactose intolerance. | Refined Wheat Flour (Maida): Contains gluten; relevant only for those with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. | Spices and Condiments: None identified. | Iodised Salt: High sodium intake is linked to elevated blood pressure risk; WHO recommends under 5g salt/day. | Acidity Regulator (330): None identified. | Cheese Powder: Blends using added starch/vegetable oil alongside real cheese have a different fat/protein profile than 100% dairy cheese. | Tomato Powder: None identified. | Anticaking Agent (551): No acute concern; EFSA flagged a data gap on nanoparticle characterisation rather than a proven hazard. | Colour (160c): No safety concerns identified. | Vegetable Oil (Corn, Soybean, and/or Sunflower Oil): Higher in unsaturated fat than palm oil; generally considered a favourable substitute for saturated fats. | Maltodextrin: None identified. | Monosodium Glutamate: Classified Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS) by FDA and reviewed favourably by JECFA/EFSA; a small subset of sensitive individuals report transient symptoms (headache, flushing) after large intakes. | Partially Hydrogenated Soybean and Cottonseed Oil: Industrial trans fats are strongly linked to increased cardiovascular disease risk; WHO has called for their global elimination. | Disodium Phosphate: High total dietary phosphate intake (from many processed foods combined) is a concern flagged for people with chronic kidney disease; not a general population risk at typical intakes. | Natural and Artificial Flavor: None identified; standard flavouring practice, though labelling terminology (natural vs nature-identical vs artificial) differs by market. | Artificial Color (Yellow 6, Yellow 5, Red 40): Some studies (e.g., the 2007 Southampton study funded by the UK FSA) have associated these dyes with hyperactivity in sensitive children, prompting the EU/UK warning label; evidence remains debated and the FDA has not found cause for a US ban. | Sugar: High intake linked to obesity and dental caries; WHO recommends limiting free sugars. | Disodium Inosinate: Reviewed as safe by JECFA; not linked to specific health warnings.
consumer_note: Corn: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Edible Vegetable Oil (Palmolein): Allowed everywhere; the choice of palm oil versus other vegetable oils is a manufacturer cost decision, not a regulatory requirement. | Seasoning: Milk Solids: No regulatory concerns; standard dairy ingredient, must be declared as an allergen. | Refined Wheat Flour (Maida): Allowed everywhere; must be declared as a gluten-containing allergen on labels in most jurisdictions. | Spices and Condiments: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Iodised Salt: Allowed in India but restricted in some countries' voluntary reformulation programmes due to concerns over excessive sodium consumption. | Acidity Regulator (330): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Cheese Powder: No safety concern; a labelling-transparency and recipe-composition difference rather than a regulatory ban. | Tomato Powder: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Anticaking Agent (551): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Colour (160c): No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Vegetable Oil (Corn, Soybean, and/or Sunflower Oil): No regulatory concerns identified. | Maltodextrin: No regulatory concerns identified. | Monosodium Glutamate: Allowed in India (and worldwide) but India is one of the few countries that still requires a specific pregnancy/infant caution label on MSG-containing products. | Partially Hydrogenated Soybean and Cottonseed Oil: This ingredient, as listed, looks outdated: most major markets (including the US since 2018) have effectively eliminated added industrial trans fat from packaged foods, so a modern product ingredient list containing this exact wording should be independently re-checked against the current label. | Disodium Phosphate: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities for the general population. | Natural and Artificial Flavor: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities. | Artificial Color (Yellow 6, Yellow 5, Red 40): Allowed in India and most of the world, but the EU/UK require a child-hyperactivity warning label on food containing these dyes - a label India does not currently mandate. | Sugar: No ban anywhere; a conventional sweetener, though nutrition guidance recommends moderating total added-sugar intake. | Disodium Inosinate: No major regulatory concerns have been identified by leading food safety authorities.

key_differences: India's Kinder Joy (per Kinder India's official ingredient panel) lists three separate, partly unnamed fat sources -Palmolein, Palm Oil, and a generic 'Edible Vegetable Fat'- and does not declare any hazelnut-type flavouring. The Middle East version (per Kinder ME's official panel) names its fats specifically as 'Palm, Sal' and explicitly lists 'Natural Identical Hazelnut Type' flavouring alongside vanillin, giving it a more hazelnut-forward flavour profile than the India version.
notable_additives: Generic 'Edible Vegetable Fat' (India, criticised by foodnetindia for lack of specificity); Named Sal fat, hazelnut-type flavouring (Middle East)
allergens: Milk, Gluten, Soy (India, explicitly declared); Milk, Wheat, Soy (Middle East)
regulatory_difference: FSSAI permits use of the general term 'edible vegetable fat' without naming the specific source oil, unlike EU/Gulf-region labelling conventions reflected in the Middle East panel, which name the specific fat sources (palm, sal) individually.
health_note: The lack of hazelnut-type flavouring in India's Kinder Joy formulation (versus the Middle East version) is a commonly noted taste difference by consumers; from a labelling-transparency standpoint, India's use of an unspecified 'edible vegetable fat' makes it harder for consumers to assess the specific fat source they are consuming.
source: Kinder India official product page (kinder.com/in/en/kinder-joy), Kinder Middle East official product page (kinder.com/me/en/kinder-joy), 1mg.com India ingredient listing, foodnetindia.in Kinder Joy review, Kinder UK 'Fats and Oils' ingredient glossary
ingredient_explanation: Sugar [Sugar (Sucrose)]: Cane or beet-derived sweetener. | Palmolein [Palm Oil / Palmolein]: Vegetable oil/fat pressed from the fruit of the oil palm; palmolein is its liquid fraction. | Skimmed Cow Milk Powder (19.5%) [Milk Solids / Milk Powder]: Dried milk components (protein, lactose, fat) used to add dairy richness. | Low Fat Cocoa Powder (4%) [Cocoa Solids / Cocoa Mass / Cocoa Butter]: Components derived from roasted cocoa beans. | Wheat Flour [Wheat Flour / Maida (refined wheat flour)]: Milled wheat grain, refined (maida) or whole; provides starch and gluten structure. | Toasted Wheat Germ [Toasted Wheat Germ / Wheat Starch]: Nutrient-dense wheat embryo (germ) or its starch fraction, used for texture and toasted flavour in wafer-based confectionery. | Powdered Barley Malt Extract [Malt Flavouring / Barley Malt Extract]: A flavouring syrup made from sprouted, dried barley, giving a toasty-sweet note (used in cereals and wafers). | Emulsifier (Lecithin – INS 322) [Emulsifier (Lecithin – INS 322)]: A conventional food ingredient; specific per-country additive regulation does not typically apply. | Sunflowerseed Oil [Vegetable Oil (Corn/Soybean/Canola/Sunflower blend)]: A blend of seed oils used as a frying/cooking medium. | Emulsifiers (Lecithins: Soy/Sunflower) [Lecithin (Soy/Sunflower) - INS/E322]: A natural fat-derived emulsifier that helps oil and water-based ingredients blend smoothly. | Flavourings (Natural Identical Vanillin, Natural Identical Hazelnut Type) [Natural Vanilla Extract / Natural-Identical Flavouring / Natural & Artificial Flavours]: Flavouring compounds - either extracted from vanilla pods, chemically identical copies of natural flavour molecules, or wholly synthetic flavour blends.
